original_hypothesis.title: Broken onlyOwner/onlyManager Modifiers Enable Complete Protocol Takeover
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Security.sol modifiers onlyOwner and onlyManager evaluate boolean expressions as no-op statements (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`) without require/revert. Every function guarded by these modifiers is effectively publicly callable by any address. An attacker can call transferOwnership to become owner, setManager to become manager, setProtocolFeePercent to redirect all protocol fees, setFeeRedistributor to a malicious contract, and setERC20Factory to deploy backdoored tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental access control failure affecting all privileged operations across Curves.sol and FeeSplitter.sol. The modifiers compile without error but provide zero protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: modifier onlyOwner() { msg.sender == owner; _; } — boolean expression as statement with no require/if-revert, compiles as no-op

preconditions: Any external address can call these functions
No additional guards exist in the modifier bodies

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boolean expression as statement in modifier body
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: msg.sender == owner evaluates to true/false but result is discarded; execution continues to _;

analysis_questions: Can Solidity 0.8.7 compiler warn about unused expression results in modifiers?
Are there any other guards in the call chain that might compensate?
What is the complete list of exploitable functions?
validation_requirements: Call transferOwnership from non-owner address and verify success
Call setProtocolFeePercent from non-owner and verify state change

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls transferOwnership(attacker), then setProtocolFeePercent(1 ether, attacker) to set 100% protocol fee to their address. All subsequent trades route ETH to attacker.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol fund drainage — all future trade fees, ability to deploy malicious factory/redistributor

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying onlyOwner/onlyManager actually revert for unauthorized callers

validation_result.mechanism: Broken access control modifiers (no-op boolean expressions)
validation_result.impact_summary: Complete protocol takeover. Any address can bypass access controls to become owner/manager, redirect all protocol fees, and manipulate fee distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `Security.sol` contract defines two custom access control modifiers: `onlyOwner` and `onlyManager`. However, instead of using `require(msg.sender == owner, "...");` or an `if (!...) revert();` pattern, the modifiers simply evaluate a boolean expression (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`). In Solidity, evaluating a boolean expression without a conditional statement or assignment is a valid statement but has no effect on execution flow (it is a no-op). Consequently, the modifiers never revert, and execution always proceeds to `_;`. This effectively makes every function protected by these modifiers publicly callable by any address.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in `Security.sol` lines 8-15. The boolean expressions are evaluated but their results are discarded, providing zero access control.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The expression `msg.sender == owner;` evaluates to true or false, but because it is not wrapped in a `require()` statement, it does not halt execution if the caller is not the owner. Execution continues to `_;`.
Similar to `onlyOwner`, the expression `managers[msg.sender] == true;` is a no-op. Any caller bypasses the intended manager check.

calling_sequence: `msg.sender == owner;` evaluates to false but does not revert
`owner = owner_;` executes, granting the attacker ownership
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Because the modifier fails to revert unauthorized calls, the state change inside the protected function executes unconditionally.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker can execute a complete protocol takeover in a single transaction block. First, they call `transferOwnership(attacker)` to become the owner. Then, they call `setProtocolFeePercent(1 ether, attacker)` to route 100% of protocol fees to themselves. They can also call `setFeeRedistributor(malicious_contract)` to steal holder fees, or call `setERC20Factory(malicious_factory)` to ensure all future ERC20 representations are backdoored.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. The entire access control system of the protocol is non-functional. All privileged operations are exposed to the public, leading to inevitable and total loss of protocol funds, user fees, and system integrity.
recommended_mitigations: Wrap the boolean expressions in `require` statements: `require(msg.sender == owner, "Not owner");` and `require(managers[msg.sender], "Not manager");`.
Alternatively, use standard, well-tested libraries like OpenZeppelin's `Ownable` and `AccessControl` instead of writing custom access control logic.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy `Security.sol` (or `Curves.sol` which inherits it). From an address that is NOT the deployer, call `transferOwnership(non_owner_address)`. Assert that the call succeeds and the `owner` state variable has been updated to the new address.
